back_translation: The whole assembly then rose up, and together they led him to Pilate.
They then began to accuse him, saying, 'We found this man inciting our nation, forbidding the paying of tribute to Caesar, and claiming himself to be the Christ, a king.'
Pilate then asked him, 'Are you the king of the Jews?' He answered and said, 'You say so.'
Pilate then said to the chief priests and to the crowds, 'I find no fault in this man at all.'
But they kept insisting firmly, saying, 'He stirs up the people by teaching throughout the region of Judea, beginning from Galilee even to here.'
When Pilate heard this, he asked whether the man was a Galilean.
And when he learned for certain that Jesus was in Herod's jurisdiction, he sent him to Herod, who was also in Jerusalem during that time.
When Herod saw Jesus, he rejoiced greatly, because he had heard about him for a long time and wanted to meet him at least once, hoping to see some sign performed by him.
Herod asked him many questions, but he did not answer anything at all.
The chief priests and the scribes stood there, vehemently accusing him.
Herod and his soldiers despised and mocked him, and then put a luxurious robe on him to ridicule him; then they sent him back to Pilate.
On that very day, Herod and Pilate became friends with each other, for previously the two had been enemies.
Pilate then summoned the chief priests, the rulers, and the people to assemble.

And he said to them, 'You brought this man to me, accusing him of inciting the people to rebel. Behold, I have examined him in your presence but found no fault in him according to what you accuse him.'
'And Herod did not find any either, so he sent him back to us. Behold, he has done nothing deserving death.'
'Therefore I will flog him and release him.'
But they all shouted together, saying, 'Away with this man! Release Barabbas to us instead.'
This Barabbas had been imprisoned because of starting a riot in the city and murder.
Pilate, wanting to release Jesus, spoke to them again.
But they shouted back, 'Crucify him! Crucify him!'
Pilate said to them a third time, 'What wrong has this man done? I have found no fault in him deserving death. Therefore I will flog him and release him.'
But they pressed with loud shouts demanding him to be crucified, and their shouts prevailed.
So Pilate decided that what they demanded should be granted.
Pilate released the man who had been imprisoned for rioting and murder, as they had asked, but handed Jesus over to their will.
As they were leading him away, the soldiers conscripted a certain Simon of Cyrene who was coming in from the countryside, and made him carry the cross behind Jesus.
A great crowd of people was following him, including women who were beating their breasts and lamenting aloud for him.
Jesus turned back and said to them, 'Daughters of Jerusalem, do not weep for me. Weep rather for yourselves and for your children.
Behold, the time is coming when people will say, "Blessed are the barren women, and the wombs that never bore, and the breasts that never nursed."
At that time people will begin to say to the mountains, "Fall on us," and to the hills, "Cover us."
For if they do these things when the tree is still green, what will happen when the tree is dry?'
There were also two other criminals being led away with him to be executed.
When they came to the place called 'The Skull,' they crucified him there, together with the criminals, one on the right and the other on the left.
⟦Jesus said, 'Father, forgive them, for they do not know what they are doing.'⟧ The soldiers cast lots to divide his clothes among themselves.
The people stood watching, while the rulers sneered at him, saying, 'He saved others; if he is the Christ, the Chosen One of God, let him save himself.'
The soldiers also mocked him, coming forward and offering him sour wine.
They said, 'If you are the King of the Jews, save yourself.'
There was an inscription written above him: 'This one is the King of the Jews.'
One of the criminals hanging there insulted him, saying, 'Are you not the Christ? Then save yourself and us as well!'
But the other rebuked him, saying, 'Do you not fear God, since you are under the same judgment?'
'As for us, it is just, for we are receiving the penalty for what we did. But this man has done nothing wrong.'
Then he said, 'Lord Jesus, remember me when you come into your kingdom.'
Jesus answered him, 'Truly I tell you, today you will be with me in paradise.'
It was now about noon, and darkness came over the whole land until three in the afternoon.
The sun was darkened, and the curtain of the temple was torn in two.
Jesus cried out in a loud voice, 'Father, into your hands I commit my spirit.' Having said this, he died.
When the centurion saw what had happened, he glorified God, saying, 'Truly this man was righteous.'
When all the crowds that had gathered to see the spectacle had observed what happened, they beat their breasts and returned home.
All those who knew him, together with the women who had followed him from Galilee, stood at a distance watching these things.
Behold, there was a man named Joseph, a member of the council, a good and righteous man,
who had not agreed with the decision and action of the others. Joseph was from the city of Arimathea in Judea, and was waiting for the kingdom of God.
Joseph went to Pilate and asked for the body of Jesus.
He took the body down from the cross, wrapped it in a fine linen cloth, and laid the body in a tomb cut out of rock, where no one had yet been buried.
It was the day of Preparation, and the Sabbath was about to begin.
The women who had followed him from Galilee followed along and saw the tomb and how his body was laid.
After returning, they prepared spices and fragrant oils; then on the Sabbath they rested according to the commandment.
